title: Alternative History of the German Invasion

jokes.text: I hear the final is a sundae bar.
jokes.setup: Discussion about the History of Ice Cream class final exam
jokes.punchline: Literal ice cream sundae bar instead of traditional test
jokes.explanation: Clean wordplay on 'sundae' vs 'Sunday' with thematic connection to ice cream class. Economical and logical.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: So what's the final exam for History of Ice Cream?

dialogue.speaker: Professor
dialogue.line: There is no exam. We're having an ice cream sundae bar instead.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Wait, that's it? No questions, no essays?

dialogue.speaker: Professor
dialogue.line: Nope. Just toppings and sprinkles.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: This is the worst timeline.

jokes.text: I don't get history. If I wanted to know what happened in Europe a long time ago, I'd watch Game of Thrones.
jokes.setup: Troy expressing confusion about studying history
jokes.punchline: Conflating fantasy TV show with actual European history
jokes.explanation: Perfect Troy character moment - his naive logic feels genuine to the character while being absurd. Strong setup-punchline structure.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're telling me Game of Thrones isn't actually about what happened in Europe?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: It's a fictional show, Troy.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: But... but the dragons seemed so historically accurate.

jokes.text: Troy, that show takes place in a fantasy realm. This class is about the real world. - [Gasps] - Not the show. You need to think before you speak.
jokes.setup: Correction that Game of Thrones is fantasy, not real history
jokes.punchline: Troy's shocked gasp at learning there's a real world beyond the show
jokes.explanation: Escalates the previous joke perfectly. Troy's gasp and the correction show his complete disconnect from reality.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Wait, Game of Thrones isn't real?

jokes.text: He's British, and he has a degree.
jokes.setup: Describing why the teacher is intimidating
jokes.punchline: Treating basic qualifications as rare/impressive at Greendale
jokes.explanation: Perfect Greendale joke - having a degree is noteworthy qualification. Economical and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: He's intimidating because he actually has a master's degree and knows what he's talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: At Greendale, that's basically a superpower.

jokes.text: I can't have a hard-buns teacher.
jokes.setup: Mishearing 'hard-ass' teacher
jokes.punchline: Shirley's innocent mispronunciation to avoid profanity
jokes.explanation: Classic Shirley character moment - avoiding curse words in her own way. Predictable but well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Professor, I heard you were a real hard-ass teacher.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: I mean... a real hard-*bottom* teacher.

jokes.text: Quick impression. 'Wah.' Who am I? You guys. Ha! Dead on.
jokes.setup: Pierce offers to do an impression
jokes.punchline: Generic baby cry representing the group complaining
jokes.explanation: Perfect Pierce - dismissive of others while being proud of lazy impression. Structure works well with his self-satisfaction.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Check this out. This is you guys complaining.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Waaah! Waaah! Waaah!

jokes.text: We might actually learn so...n of a bitch! Germans: [Imitating techno tune]
jokes.setup: Optimistic speech about learning being cut off
jokes.punchline: Sudden appearance of annoying Germans with techno sounds
jokes.explanation: Good interruption timing creates contrast between optimism and immediate annoyance. Sets up episode conflict.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Learning is a journey, not a destination. Every day we grow, we expand our horizons, we become better versions of—

dialogue.speaker: German 1
dialogue.line: Hallo!

dialogue.speaker: German 2
dialogue.line: Ve are here to study!

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Oh, come on!

jokes.text: I thought they transferred after they lost their foosball scholarship.
jokes.setup: Wondering why the Germans are back
jokes.punchline: Foosball scholarship as a real thing at Greendale
jokes.explanation: Perfect Greendale absurdity - treating foosball scholarships as normal. Economical world-building humor.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait, why are the Germans back?

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: We offered them a foosball scholarship.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: A foosball scholarship? That's a real thing at Greendale?

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: We're a very progressive institution.

jokes.text: I heard they were juicing.
jokes.setup: Following up on foosball scholarship loss
jokes.punchline: Performance-enhancing drugs for foosball
jokes.explanation: Nice escalation of the foosball absurdity. Takes the premise one logical step further into ridiculousness.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait, you're saying there are performance-enhancing drugs for foosball?

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Apparently it's a huge problem in competitive foosball circles.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: That's actually a real thing. Professional foosball players have been caught using stimulants.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: So you're telling me people cheat at a table game? What's next, steroids for air hockey?

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I've been taking performance-enhancing drugs for years. They're called scotch.

jokes.text: No, them leaving would mean Greendale got slightly better, which, as we know, does not happen.
jokes.setup: Questioning why the Germans would leave
jokes.punchline: Self-aware commentary on Greendale never improving
jokes.explanation: Classic Abed meta-commentary. Perfect character voice delivering show-aware observation with deadpan precision.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: The Germans left because they realized Greendale never actually improves. We're trapped in a cycle of incompetence. It's basically a TV show where nothing ever really changes or gets better.

jokes.text: If there is one thing Germans don't do, it's hold a grudge. Unless we're talking about Die Hard 3. Or the 20th century.
jokes.setup: Germans don't hold grudges
jokes.punchline: Exception for movie sequel and entire century of world wars
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from trivial (movie) to massive (world wars). Dark historical humor balanced with absurd specificity of Die Hard 3.

dialogue.speaker: Reinhold
dialogue.line: Germans don't hold grudges, except for movie sequels and entire centuries of world wars.

jokes.text: I know what you're all thinking, and the answer is... Yes. I am a direct descendant of general Sir Charles Cornwallis, who surrendered the British forces at Yorktown. Was anyone thinking that? Students: No.
jokes.setup: Professor assumes students are thinking about his ancestry
jokes.punchline: Universal 'no' from students who weren't thinking that at all
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection setup with collective denial. Shows professor's self-importance vs. student indifference perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Cornwallis
dialogue.line: I suppose you're all wondering about my distinguished ancestry.

dialogue.speaker: Students
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: According to Oxford University, it means that my 20 years' loyal service there means nothing next to a little slipup with a co-ed.
jokes.setup: Explaining what 'history is written by the victors' means
jokes.punchline: Reveals inappropriate relationship that got him fired
jokes.explanation: Uncomfortable oversharing that explains why he's at Greendale. Character-building through inappropriate revelation.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Cornwallis
dialogue.line: History is written by the victors, which is why nobody knows about my relationship with that student.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Cornwallis
dialogue.line: Well, nobody knew. Until I got fired and had to explain it to everyone.

jokes.text: I'm confused. When do we get our ice cream?
jokes.setup: Student confusion during serious history lecture
jokes.punchline: Still expecting ice cream from the wrong class
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to opening ice cream discussion. Shows student confusion and maintains running theme.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait, this is History 101, not Calculus?

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Jeff, we've been in this class for three weeks.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Right, right. So no ice cream truck?

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: That was the Chemistry class. Last semester.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Okay, but the professor said something about a 'cool' field trip...

jokes.text: [Ominous music] [Laughing evilly] I was recalling a very funny episode of the German version of The Nanny. 'Franlein' gets a... It wouldn't translate.
jokes.setup: Ominous music and evil laughter suggesting sinister plotting
jokes.punchline: Just remembering German sitcom humor that doesn't translate
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection using film language. Sets up sinister expectation then deflates with mundane German TV reference. Well-structured comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Germans
dialogue.line: Just remembering German sitcom humor that doesn't translate

jokes.text: And I still have those pants. True story.
jokes.setup: Random mid-conversation revelation
jokes.punchline: Unnecessary emphasis on trivial pants information
jokes.explanation: Random character moment that feels natural but adds little comedic value. Mildly amusing non-sequitur.

dialogue.line: Unnecessary emphasis on trivial pants information

jokes.text: [High-pitched scream] Sorry. I should've realized that might be startling.
jokes.setup: Doctor makes sudden high-pitched scream
jokes.punchline: Casual apology for obviously startling behavior
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy followed by understated reaction. The casual apology contrasts well with the extreme behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Ken Kedan
dialogue.line: Sorry about that. I just get excited when I see a new perirectal fissure.

jokes.text: 'Hello, my name is Kevin. I have Changnesia'? What is that?
jokes.setup: Reading Chang's medical note
jokes.punchline: Nonsense medical condition combining Chang's name with amnesia
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character-specific wordplay. Creates memorable fake condition that becomes central to episode. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff Winger
dialogue.line: What does this say? 'Changesia'? Is that a real thing?

dialogue.speaker: Dr. Ken Kedan
dialogue.line: It's a rare condition. Chang loses his memory, but only about being Chang.

jokes.text: No, he's always Dean that.
jokes.setup: Doctor explaining Chang's condition
jokes.punchline: Dean's name-pun showing Chang always mixes words
jokes.explanation: Classic Dean pun behavior. Shows Chang's word-mixing isn't new while displaying Dean's compulsive punning.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: I'm afraid Mr. Chang has a severe case of dyslexia. He frequently confuses words and their meanings.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: In fact, just this morning he called me 'Dean Phelton' and asked if I was feeling okay.

jokes.text: I can't have an unbalanced nut job traipsing around on my campus! Put that on the rack. Put that in the fridge. Get him out of here!
jokes.setup: Dean rejecting Chang's presence
jokes.punchline: Mixing campus management with domestic organization tasks
jokes.explanation: Shows Dean's scattered thinking mixing administrative and household tasks. Character-appropriate nervous breakdown.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Mr. Chang, I'm going to have to ask you to leave my office. You're not a student, you're not faculty, and frankly, your presence here is making me very uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: But Dean, I just wanted to—

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: No. Out. And on your way out, could you organize the filing cabinets? They're a disaster. And maybe straighten up the supply closet? My wife's been on me about keeping things neat at home, and I find it really helps to practice here first.

jokes.text: [High-pitched wail] I just felt a strange disturbance. Did you have dairy this morning? Could be that. Or it could be that great evil is nearby.
jokes.setup: Pierce sensing supernatural disturbance
jokes.punchline: Deflating mystical moment with lactose intolerance explanation
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection from mystical to mundane. Pierce's character perfectly balances new-age pretension with bodily functions.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I sense a supernatural disturbance in this room. The spirits are restless, the veil between worlds grows thin...

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Wait. No, that's just the leftover cheese dip I had at lunch. I'm lactose intolerant.

jokes.text: Why must he hurt so with his vords? He's like a less funny Hans Rickles.
jokes.setup: German student hurt by insult
jokes.punchline: Combining Don Rickles with German name/accent
jokes.explanation: Clever combination of insult comedian reference with German accent/names. Shows character attempting American cultural references.

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: You know, in Germany we have a saying: 'A man who insults without wit is like a sausage without mustard.'

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well in America we have a guy named Don Rickles who made millions doing exactly that.

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: Ah, so you Americans celebrate the talentless? How very... American of you.

jokes.text: Someone must have changed the channel to USA, 'cause I just watched a burn notice.
jokes.setup: Responding to German's hurt feelings
jokes.punchline: TV network pun combining USA Network and 'burn notice' show/concept
jokes.explanation: Competent pun but relies heavily on pop culture knowledge. Works structurally but feels somewhat forced.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Come on, German. You didn't get burned. You got USA Network'd.

jokes.text: Because that's called appeasement. And everyone knows if you give the Germans something small, like the study room or Austria, they end up wanting something big, like Earth.
jokes.setup: Explaining why they can't give Germans the table
jokes.punchline: Escalating from study room to Austria to Earth via WWII references
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from trivial to historical to absurd. Perfect Jeff rhetoric mixing history knowledge with paranoid logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: We can't give them the table because it'll escalate. First it's the study room, then it's Austria, then it's the whole Earth.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: That's how it started last time.

jokes.text: If there were more people in the world like Annie, we'd all be speaking vietnamese right now. - [Gasps in shock] - That was my war. I had flashbacks for years. Pierce, you moved to Canada.
jokes.setup: Jeff's historical analogy about Annie's appeasement
jokes.punchline: Pierce's fake Vietnam trauma exposed by his Canada draft dodging
jokes.explanation: Perfect Pierce moment - fake war trauma immediately debunked. Great character consistency with his tendency to lie about military service.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Your appeasement strategy is like Neville Chamberlain in 1938.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: That's not fair. I'm trying to keep the peace.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: You know, that reminds me of when I was in Vietnam. Toughest years of my life.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Pierce, you fled to Canada in 1969 to avoid the draft.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: That was... a strategic retreat.

jokes.text: Those people call ham 'bacon'.
jokes.setup: Pierce defending his Canada experience as hell
jokes.punchline: Trivial food terminology as evidence of Canadian horror
jokes.explanation: Perfect Pierce logic - minor cultural difference treated as major trauma. Shows his shallow understanding of real hardship.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Canada was a nightmare! They call bacon 'back bacon' — BACK bacon! And they say 'double-double' for coffee instead of just... a coffee! It's chaos!

jokes.text: So today, ve study in here. Unt tonight, we dine in hell. [Imitating techno beat]
jokes.setup: Germans claiming victory in study room
jokes.punchline: 300 movie quote followed by techno music imitation
jokes.explanation: Pop culture reference combined with established German techno trait. Functional but relies on external references.

dialogue.speaker: German Student 1
dialogue.line: We have conquered the study room!

dialogue.speaker: German Student 2
dialogue.line: This is our territory now.

dialogue.speaker: German Student 1
dialogue.line: Tonight, we dine in hell!

dialogue.speaker: German Student 2
dialogue.line: Boots up! Boots up!

dialogue.speaker: German Students
dialogue.line: Chk chk chk chk chk chk! Wub wub wub wub!

jokes.text: Doktor Blitz, knight of the Atlantis Guild, wielder of the four-pronged trident on SpawnCraft? How did you know? I recognized your catchphrase, 'Tonight we dine in hell!' I'm SpaceTimer8032.
jokes.setup: Abed recognizing Karl's gaming identity
jokes.punchline: 300 quote is actually gaming catchphrase, not movie reference
jokes.explanation: Brilliant recontextualization of the 300 quote as gaming catchphrase. Perfect Abed moment recognizing gaming culture.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Wait, '300'? That's not a movie reference for you, is it?

dialogue.speaker: Karl
dialogue.line: No, it's my gaming catchphrase.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Of course it is.

jokes.text: That man saved my life. In a video game.
jokes.setup: Abed explaining Karl saved his life
jokes.punchline: Clarification that it was only in a video game
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection with economical delivery. Shows Abed's tendency to treat gaming as seriously as real life.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Karl saved my life.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Well, in a video game.

jokes.text: Oh, now he has me saying it. Amdeansia. Amdeansia... I'm all turned around.
jokes.setup: Dean frustrated with Chang's made-up condition
jokes.punchline: Creating his own Dean-based amnesia portmanteau
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dean character moment - his compulsive name-punning even when frustrated. Shows wordplay spreading like virus.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: I'm not falling for this again, Chang. There's no such thing as 'Changnesia.'

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Fine. You know what? I'm going to create my own condition. It's called 'Pelton's Selective Memory Disorder' or as I like to call it... 'Peltesia.'

jokes.text: Hey, apropos of nothing, what's that sound you make when you see something shocking? - [Gasps in shock] - Oh, yeah, that's it.
jokes.setup: Jeff asking innocent question about Annie's reaction
jokes.punchline: Setting up Annie to discover Germans at their table
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Annie's predictable reaction. Jeff's manipulation feels natural and the structure works well.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Why are you acting so weird?

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: I'm not acting weird. There are just some Germans at that table over there, and I think they're staring at us.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: So what? Let them stare.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: You don't understand. Germans are very efficient. If they're staring at us, they're probably analyzing us, and if they're analyzing us, they're probably judging us.

jokes.text: I am afraid it is you who must do the making like a tree, which is to say, leaving.
jokes.setup: German telling study group to leave
jokes.punchline: Over-explaining the 'make like a tree and leave' idiom
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate ESL moment where German student over-explains American idiom. Shows cultural translation difficulty.

dialogue.speaker: German Student
dialogue.line: You must leave. Make like a tree and leave.

dialogue.speaker: German Student
dialogue.line: This is an idiom. The tree, it does not actually leave. It is rooted. But metaphorically, you are like the tree, and you must go. The 'leaving' is the action you must perform, separate from the tree's inability to leave.

dialogue.speaker: German Student
dialogue.line: Do you understand? You are the leaves. No, wait. You must become leaves and fall from the tree of this room. No, that is not correct either.

dialogue.speaker: German Student
dialogue.line: Just go.

jokes.text: You're asking for our papers? I thought this was America, not Arizona.
jokes.setup: Germans being asked for identification
jokes.punchline: Germans lecturing Americans about papers and Arizona immigration laws
jokes.explanation: Sharp political irony - Germans referencing American immigration policies. Historical awareness makes the joke more pointed.

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: We have papers. Always we have papers. Is very important in Germany.

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: You Americans, you should require papers like we do. Very organized. Very efficient.

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: Arizona, they understand this. They know papers are necessary.

jokes.text: It would appear that in this battle, our bulge was a little bit bigger. [Chuckles] Guten bye-bye.
jokes.setup: Germans celebrating their victory
jokes.punchline: Battle of the Bulge reference with sexual innuendo and German goodbye
jokes.explanation: Historical reference with juvenile double entendre. The German goodbye pun is nice touch but overall relies on crude humor.

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: We are celebrating our great victory!

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: Like the Battle of the Bulge, but this time we came prepared.

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: Auf Wiedersehen!

jokes.text: It's like a Darren Aronofsky film.
jokes.setup: Describing their awful new study room
jokes.punchline: Comparing bad lighting to art house director's aesthetic
jokes.explanation: Clever film reference connecting harsh lighting to Aronofsky's visual style. Shows character's cultural knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: The lighting in here is terrible.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: That's not terrible lighting. That's Terrence Malick.

jokes.text: All: [Screaming] Agh! Everybody grab me, please! Ugh! - Nobody grab him. - [Screaming] Pierce, just let go. You'd like that, wouldn't you? Of the thing, Pierce.
jokes.setup: Pierce getting electrocuted trying to fix lights
jokes.punchline: Pierce's paranoid interpretation of 'let go' advice while being shocked
jokes.explanation: Perfect Pierce paranoia even during electrocution. Physical comedy enhanced by character-specific interpretation.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Let go? Let go?! That's what they said on the Donner Party!

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: I'm not dying out here so you people can eat me!

jokes.text: [Air hissing] Oh, no. - Oh! - Ugh! [Somber music] [Maniacal laughter]
jokes.setup: Study group's room completely falling apart
jokes.punchline: Germans' evil laughter at their misfortune contrasted with somber music
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy sequence with musical contrast. The escalating disaster works well with villain laughter.

dialogue.speaker: Germans
dialogue.line: Mwahahaha!

dialogue.speaker: Germans
dialogue.line: Mwahahaha!

jokes.text: If that's true, then Chang, who is me, deserves to go to jail.
jokes.setup: Chang processing information about his past crimes
jokes.punchline: Awkward self-reference confusion while accepting guilt
jokes.explanation: Shows Chang's confusion about his identity while maintaining the amnesia act. Grammatically awkward in character-appropriate way.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: So you're saying I've committed crimes? Multiple crimes?

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: Well, yes, obviously I have. I'm me. I know what I've done.

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: Wait, are you asking me to confess to things I already know about myself, or are you telling me about crimes I forgot I committed?

dialogue.speaker: Chang
dialogue.line: Because those are two very different levels of disturbing.

jokes.text: And that failed ventriloquist's name was Slobodan Milosevic.
jokes.setup: Professor ending what seems like a harmless anecdote
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to Yugoslav war criminal as punchline
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor combining innocent setup with war criminal reference. Perfectly inappropriate for academic setting.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Cornwallis
dialogue.line: ...and that's why I always tell my students, 'Never trust a man who says he's just passing through.' Reminds me of my old friend Radovan Karadžić.

jokes.text: Guten luck on the examination. Ve vill be preparing for it in study room 'F', as in 'Fictory'.
jokes.setup: Germans wishing study group luck on exam
jokes.punchline: Mispronouncing 'victory' with German accent while being smug
jokes.explanation: Accent-based wordplay that shows character attempting English idioms. Mildly clever but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: Good luck on your exam. You vill achieve victory!

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: Vell, actually, it is pronounced 'viktory' in German. Much more efficient.

jokes.text: I like the way your old chair cradles mein knaidels. [Laughter] I just made that up. You know, English is my second language.
jokes.setup: German making crude joke about testicles and chair
jokes.punchline: Proud of improvised dirty joke, then explaining language barrier
jokes.explanation: Crude humor with ESL excuse. Character-appropriate but relies on juvenile content rather than cleverness.

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: So I'm sitting in the chair, and I say, 'My testicles are like the legs of the chair - they support everything!' Ha! Very funny, yes?

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: I am very proud of this joke. I made it up myself.

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: In German, it is even funnier because 'Hoden' sounds like 'Hose,' which means 'pants,' so it's a double meaning that doesn't translate...

jokes.text: Many years ago, before the concept of 'too soon' existed, there was a show about a bunch of goofy Nazis running a prison camp, and that show was called... - Hogan's Heroes? - Hogan's Heroes. - Yes.
jokes.setup: Pierce building up to TV show reference
jokes.punchline: Others finishing his obvious reference while acknowledging show's inappropriate premise
jokes.explanation: Meta-commentary on problematic TV history while setting up episode's Nazi parallel. Shows awareness of show's dated content.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: You know what this reminds me of? That show where the guy...

dialogue.speaker: Others
dialogue.line: Yeah, we know what you're going to say, and yes, the premise is problematic.

jokes.text: And while, for a latchkey kid with no jewish friends, it was a bit desensitizing, it still taught me that the lovable misfits always win. And the bumbling Germans always lose.
jokes.setup: Pierce reflecting on Hogan's Heroes influence
jokes.punchline: Admitting the show's problematic impact on his worldview
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment showing Pierce's casual racism origins. Self-aware about problematic influences while maintaining character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: You know, Hogan's Heroes really shaped how I see the world. I thought war was just a bunch of guys in tight uniforms outsmarting each other. Turns out there's actual consequences and suffering involved. Who knew?

jokes.text: You had me at 'ruse.' That was the last thing I said. Good thing you said it.
jokes.setup: Character claiming to be sold on Jeff's plan
jokes.punchline: Jeff pointing out the timing of the 'had me at' reference
jokes.explanation: Meta-joke about the timing of movie reference clichés. Jeff's correction feels natural and adds layer to the reference.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown
dialogue.line: You had me at 'let's commit insurance fraud.'

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: We literally just started talking.

jokes.text: [Whispers] It's been banned by the FDA.
jokes.setup: Offering authentic German sausage
jokes.punchline: Treating FDA ban as selling point rather than warning
jokes.explanation: Good character logic showing American fascination with forbidden things. The whisper adds conspiratorial humor.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Ladies and gentlemen, I present to you authentic German sausage. Imported directly from Berlin.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Wait, isn't that banned by the FDA?

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Exactly. FDA banned. That means it's the real deal. You can't get this kind of authenticity in America anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Jeff, that's not a selling point.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Not a selling point? Troy, do you know how many people would kill for a banned sausage? It's exclusive.

jokes.text: That blutwurst was probably injected with a laxative to make us pull a grete waitz in our trousers.
jokes.setup: German suspecting poisoned sausage
jokes.punchline: Euphemistic German expression for defecating in pants
jokes.explanation: Cultural-specific euphemism that shows character's background. Crude humor with some originality in the expression.

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: This sausage, it tastes... strange. I think it is poisoned.

dialogue.speaker: German student
dialogue.line: Ach, I have made in my pants!

jokes.text: I promise you, there's nothing gross in this sausage. It's just pig's blood stuffed into a cow's intestine.
jokes.setup: Reassuring Germans about sausage safety
jokes.punchline: Describing disgusting ingredients while claiming it's not gross
jokes.explanation: Perfect irony - describing revolting contents while claiming normalcy. Shows disconnect between intention and execution.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff Winger
dialogue.line: Look, I know what you're thinking. 'How can we trust American sausage?' Well, let me assure you, it's made from only the finest... parts.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff Winger
dialogue.line: We take the lips, the gums, the eyeballs, the tail... we grind it all up with some mysterious pink slime, add a little texture with what I'm pretty sure is the snout...

dialogue.speaker: Jeff Winger
dialogue.line: But hey, it's not gross. It's sausage.

jokes.text: Just take that large cake back to the study room and enjoy it there. So there's nothing hiding inside this giant German chocolate cake? Ask Abed. You can trust him. He's a friend. Nope. Just solid cake.
jokes.setup: Obvious trap with suspiciously large cake
jokes.punchline: Abed's deadpan lie about cake contents to help the plan
jokes.explanation: Good tension building with obvious trap. Abed's betrayal feels surprising given his usual honesty about situations.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: It's not a trap. The cake is real. I checked it myself.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: It's just regular cake. Chocolate. Nothing suspicious.

jokes.text: Should I release the tear gas, or has that ship sank?
jokes.setup: Troy asking about their failed plan
jokes.punchline: Mixing 'that ship has sailed' with 'sank' instead of 'sailed'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Troy malapropism mixing nautical metaphors. Character-appropriate language confusion that works naturally.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: That ship has sank.

jokes.text: But you were the only German students celebrating German culture, and here at Greendale, that is a big, fat no-no. We frown on anyone celebrating their own cultural heritage. I mean, if the hasidic student union wants to host a Kwanzaa celebration, you go, Jews! Hanukkah? No, sir.
jokes.setup: Explaining why Germans are banned
jokes.punchline: Arbitrary cultural celebration rules that make no logical sense
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of arbitrary institutional rules and cultural policing. The specific examples make the absurdity more concrete.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: We have very strict rules about cultural celebrations here at Greendale. Germans are banned.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff Winger
dialogue.line: Why are Germans banned?

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Because last Oktoberfest, they got out of hand. So now we have a zero-tolerance policy on all things German.

dialogue.speaker: Britta Perry
dialogue.line: That seems a little extreme, Dean.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Well, we also banned the Irish after St. Patrick's Day, Cinco de Mayo celebrations require a permit signed by me personally, and Christmas is only allowed on alternating years.

jokes.text: As they say in Ferris Bueller, danke schoen. And as they say in the movie, gotcha! Gotcha.
jokes.setup: Germans quoting Ferris Bueller
jokes.punchline: Study group revealing their counter-trap
jokes.explanation: Good back-and-forth gotcha moment. Pop culture reference fits the escalation.

dialogue.speaker: Germans
dialogue.line: Life moves pretty fast. If you don't stop and look around once in a while, you could miss it.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: We know.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: We've seen Ferris Bueller's Day Off.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: We're from the internet generation.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: We've had access to all of human culture for years.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Your obscure reference is our Wikipedia article.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: And we don't appreciate you assuming we haven't.

jokes.text: This whole time, we thought the Germans were the Germans. But it turns out, we're the Germans.
jokes.setup: Realizing they've become the villains
jokes.punchline: Self-aware recognition of their Nazi-like behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect thematic culmination line. Economical delivery of episode's central irony with memorable phrasing.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait, are we the bad guys here?

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, we've become like... study group Nazis.

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: That's not helping your case.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: We're literally just sitting around judging people.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: The resemblance is actually cinematically striking.

jokes.text: You are wearing an S.S. t-shirt. - Oh. - Oh! - Oh! - Oh.
jokes.setup: Leonard objecting to being called Nazi
jokes.punchline: Everyone noticing his Nazi SS shirt
jokes.explanation: Perfect visual irony moment. Leonard's indignation immediately undercut by obvious Nazi imagery. Great group reaction.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: I'm not a Nazi!

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Dude, you're wearing an SS shirt.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: What? No, I'm not!

dialogue.speaker: Britta
dialogue.line: Leonard, those are literally Nazi symbols.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: Yeah, the double lightning bolts...

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: That's definitely a Nazi thing.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: Classic fascist imagery.

dialogue.speaker: Leonard
dialogue.line: Oh! SS stands for 'Scuba Squad'!

jokes.text: Hate to say it, but this is what happens when a group of people blindly follows a charismatic, golden-throated leader. Are you actually comparing me to Hitler? [Overlapping agreements] There are similarities.
jokes.setup: Shirley making political commentary about blind following
jokes.punchline: Jeff's horrified realization and group's agreement about Hitler comparison
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation of Nazi parallels to personal level. Jeff's wounded vanity vs. legitimate criticism creates good tension.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: People just blindly follow whatever they're told without thinking for themselves.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: That's a pretty broad generalization, Shirley.

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: Is it though? Look at history. People followed Hitler without question.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, I see your point, but—

dialogue.speaker: Shirley
dialogue.line: No buts. It's the same thing happening today.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Wait... are you comparing us to Nazis right now?

dialogue.speaker: Group
dialogue.line: Yeah, pretty much.

jokes.text: Nobody's even willing to consider that I might be the Hitler of this group?
jokes.setup: Pierce feeling left out of Hitler comparisons
jokes.punchline: Wanting to be considered the Nazi leader instead of Jeff
jokes.explanation: Perfect Pierce moment - jealous of even negative comparisons. Dark humor with excellent character insight.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Wait, why does Jeff always get to be Hitler? I want to be Hitler!

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Pierce, nobody wants to be Hitler.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Well I do! At least then people would take me seriously around here.

jokes.text: Why don't you call it a 'quadrident'?
jokes.setup: Abed mentioning four-pronged trident
jokes.punchline: Annie's logical naming correction
jokes.explanation: Annie's logical mind applied to gaming terminology. Minor but character-appropriate correction humor.

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: It's a four-pronged trident.

dialogue.speaker: Annie
dialogue.line: That would be a fork.

jokes.text: You clever British bastard. You set this whole thing up to teach us a lesson. Making the school pretend to hate us, to show us that one man's hero is another man's villain.
jokes.setup: Jeff thinking professor orchestrated everything
jokes.punchline: Overestimating professor's competence and Greendale's coordination
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection showing Jeff's tendency to see conspiracies. Sets up disappointment when reality is revealed.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: He orchestrated all of this. The whole thing was planned.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'm giving him way too much credit, aren't I?

jokes.text: Are you actually suggesting that a Professor at Greendale would set up an elaborate ruse just to teach seven students a lesson? - Uh, yes. - Yeah. - Yes. - That's a Wednesday.
jokes.setup: Professor questioning their assumption about elaborate teaching ruse
jokes.punchline: Group's matter-of-fact confirmation that it happens regularly
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-joke about the show's format. 'That's a Wednesday' perfectly captures how routine concept episodes are.

dialogue.speaker: Professor Cornwallis
dialogue.line: Wait, you're telling me this whole thing was an elaborate ruse?

dialogue.speaker: Group
dialogue.line: Yeah, Professor. It happens like every week.

jokes.text: - I really am Hitler. - Yeah. So it's just decided. No vote or anything. I hate this.
jokes.setup: Jeff accepting Hitler comparison
jokes.punchline: Pierce's continued frustration at being excluded from Nazi leadership
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to Pierce's earlier jealousy. His petulant response to democratic process is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: I'm not Hitler.

dialogue.speaker: Pierce
dialogue.line: Well, not with that attitude you're not.

jokes.text: Remember that people can 'Chang'. People can what? Hmm?
jokes.setup: Dean trying to make inspirational speech about change
jokes.punchline: Accidental Chang pun followed by innocent confusion
jokes.explanation: Classic Dean moment with perfect setup for Chang's return. His unconscious punning even in serious moments.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: You know, change is what makes us grow. We must embrace it, even when it seems scary. Even when it's... Chang.

dialogue.speaker: Dean Pelton
dialogue.line: Wait, why did I say that? I meant to say 'strange.' Why would I say Chang?

jokes.text: Is it inside? Is it outside? You find out. We challenge you to taste the bread. This coupon is a $5 bill.
jokes.setup: Describing Shirley's sandwich and giving gift certificate
jokes.punchline: Karl's literal observation about their 'coupon' being cash
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdist humor about podcast format and primitive business practices. Karl's literal response provides gentle punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Troy
dialogue.line: Shirley made us sandwiches and she threw in a gift certificate!

dialogue.speaker: Abed
dialogue.line: That's very generous.

dialogue.speaker: Karl
dialogue.line: That's not a gift certificate, that's cash.

tmdb_overview: The study group contends with a group of German students who take over the study room. Chang makes his return to campus.
insight: German invasion alternate-history setup sustains 80+ laughs through pure character escalation.
